stem: A 31-year-old G3P2 woman at 35 weeks and 2 days gestation presents with three days of progressively worsening right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and increasing fatigue. She has not had a seizure but has a mild bifrontal headache and no visual scotomata. Her two previous pregnancies were uncomplicated and she has no personal or family history of thrombotic microangiopathy, autoimmune disease or liver disease. Her blood pressure on three readings is 146-150 / 90-94 mmHg, she has 1+ proteinuria with a urine PCR of 42 mg/mmol, and the foetal CTG is reactive with normal growth on a scan one week ago. She has had no recent gastroenteritis, no bloody diarrhoea and no recent E. coli O157:H7 or shigatoxin-producing organism exposure. Bedside bloods show haemoglobin 96 g/L (anaemia with reticulocytosis), platelets 68 x10^9/L, ALT 285 U/L, AST 312 U/L, total bilirubin 38 micromol/L (predominantly indirect), LDH 850 U/L, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L (preserved), INR 1.1, creatinine 84 micromol/L (normal for pregnancy), glucose 5.2 mmol/L, and ammonia within the normal range. A peripheral blood film shows schistocytes, polychromasia and absent platelet clumps. ADAMTS13 activity measured urgently is 65% (well above the 10% threshold for TTP).
lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical and biochemical picture?

options.text: HELLP syndrome (haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets)
options.rationale: HELLP is diagnosed by Haemolysis (anaemia, schistocytes, raised LDH, raised indirect bilirubin), Elevated Liver enzymes (ALT/AST >70 U/L typically) and Low Platelets (<100 x10^9/L), usually with normal coagulation and normal glucose. This patient has all three features with preserved fibrinogen, normal INR, normal glucose and ADAMTS13 >10%, distinguishing it from AFLP, TTP and HUS (RANZCOG 2023; SOMANZ 2014).

options.text: Acute fatty liver of pregnancy with hepatic dysfunction
options.rationale: AFLP typically causes hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy (raised INR, low fibrinogen), hyperammonaemia and more pronounced hyperbilirubinaemia. This patient has normal glucose, normal INR and fibrinogen and normal ammonia (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura with neurological features
options.rationale: TTP is defined by ADAMTS13 activity <10%, with severe thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic haemolysis, neurological features and fever; ADAMTS13 of 65% effectively excludes TTP (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Atypical haemolytic uraemic syndrome with complement-mediated injury
options.rationale: HUS is dominated by acute kidney injury and microangiopathic haemolysis with relatively preserved transaminases; this patient has normal creatinine and very high transaminases, making HUS unlikely (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy with raised bile acids
options.rationale: ICP causes pruritus and isolated raised bile acids and modest transaminitis without thrombocytopenia or haemolysis (RANZCOG ICP statement).

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 woman at 11 weeks and 2 days gestation has a routine booking infection screen which shows Toxoplasma gondii IgM positive and IgG positive with low avidity, a pattern strongly suggesting recent primary infection within the last 12-16 weeks. She has had a new indoor kitten at home since six weeks before conception and recalls cleaning the litter tray on multiple occasions without gloves. She also recalls eating undercooked lamb skewers at a barbecue 10 weeks ago. She is asymptomatic, has no lymphadenopathy on examination and her partner is well with no symptoms. Toxoplasma PCR on maternal blood is positive. Amniocentesis is being arranged for foetal PCR at 18 weeks (consistent with the recommendation to wait at least six weeks after maternal seroconversion). Her booking ultrasound today shows a viable singleton with normal anatomy for gestation and no calcifications. She is referred to maternal-foetal medicine to discuss treatment options pending foetal status, and asks specifically whether the medication chosen now should change if foetal infection is later confirmed on amniotic fluid PCR. The maternal-foetal medicine consultant also outlines the typical vertical transmission risk by trimester and explains why the timing of investigations matters, before turning to the choice of initial pharmacotherapy.
lead_in: Which initial pharmacological strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Spiramycin 1 g orally three times daily, switching to sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid only if amniocentesis confirms foetal infection
options.rationale: Spiramycin is the first-trimester treatment of choice as it concentrates in the placenta and reduces vertical transmission without causing foetal harm. If foetal infection is confirmed (positive amniotic fluid PCR after 18 weeks), therapy switches to sulphadiazine + pyrimethamine + folinic acid to treat established foetal infection (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice; RCOG). Spiramycin reduces vertical transmission; sulphadiazine-pyrimethamine-folinic acid is reserved for confirmed foetal infection by amniotic fluid PCR at >=18 weeks.

options.text: Sulphadiazine plus pyrimethamine plus folinic acid commenced immediately for all women regardless of foetal status
options.rationale: Pyrimethamine is a folate antagonist and is teratogenic in the first trimester (neural tube defects, oral clefts); it should be avoided before 14-18 weeks. Treatment of the foetus is reserved for confirmed foetal infection (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Co-trimoxazole oral monotherapy without folinic acid or any foetal assessment as the definitive treatment
options.rationale: Co-trimoxazole has activity against Toxoplasma but is not standard first-line therapy in pregnancy; spiramycin is preferred to prevent transmission (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Azithromycin 500 mg orally daily as definitive monotherapy without spiramycin, folinic acid or referral
options.rationale: Azithromycin has weak in vitro activity but is not a recommended first-line agent in pregnancy (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Termination of pregnancy without any treatment given certain severe congenital toxoplasmosis at this gestation
options.rationale: Maternal primary infection in the first trimester carries a relatively low vertical transmission risk (10-15%) although consequences if transmitted can be severe; treatment with spiramycin substantially reduces transmission. Termination is not the default management (RCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

stem: A 28-year-old G2P0 woman with focal-onset epilepsy attends a joint obstetric-medicine/neurology pre-conception clinic three months before a planned pregnancy. She has been seizure-free for two years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily monotherapy, having previously failed levetiracetam (rash and breakthrough seizures) and carbamazepine (breakthrough seizures with intolerable somnolence) for seizure control. Her previous pregnancy ended in a first-trimester miscarriage while she was on lamotrigine monotherapy two years ago, and she has not used lamotrigine since. She has no family history of neural tube defects or congenital malformations and her BMI is 23. She is concerned about teratogenicity having read media coverage of valproate, and asks specifically about: relative major congenital malformation rates of the commonly used AEDs, the appropriate periconceptional folate dose for women on AEDs versus the general population, the likely need for therapeutic drug monitoring during pregnancy (particularly for lamotrigine if used), and which medication she should ideally be established on by the time of conception. Her neurologist has reviewed her recent MRI and EEG which remain stable, and confirms she has reliable epilepsy without evidence of progressive structural lesion. She does not drive a heavy vehicle for work and has well-controlled mood with no current depressive symptoms. She is keen to plan a pregnancy within the next six months and is willing to delay conception if a medication switch is recommended, but wants a clear plan that balances seizure control against teratogenic risk and includes the practical issue of how to monitor any new agent through pregnancy. She also asks whether to expect a dose adjustment in pregnancy and what to do about breastfeeding postpartum, and her partner asks whether she should stop driving during the cross-titration period as a safety precaution.
lead_in: Which combination of recommendations is most appropriate?

options.text: Continue sodium valproate, commence folate 0.5 mg daily, with no need for AED drug levels during pregnancy
options.rationale: Valproate carries the highest major congenital malformation rate of all AEDs (~10%) plus a 30-40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment, and is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless every alternative has failed. Folate dose for AED users is 5 mg, not 0.5 mg (Therapeutic Goods Administration; AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Switch to phenytoin before pregnancy as it carries the lowest teratogenicity profile of all the AEDs
options.rationale: Phenytoin causes foetal hydantoin syndrome and has a major malformation rate around 5-6%, higher than lamotrigine or levetiracetam (Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

options.text: Switch (under neurology) from valproate to lamotrigine or levetiracetam pre-conception, folate 5 mg daily pre-conception, and therapeutic AED level monitoring across pregnancy
options.rationale: Lamotrigine and levetiracetam have the lowest major malformation rates (~2-3%, similar to background). Valproate (~10%) is avoided in women of childbearing potential. Folate 5 mg daily is recommended for women on AEDs. Lamotrigine clearance increases substantially in pregnancy, requiring level monitoring and dose adjustment (Therapeutic Guidelines - Neurology; AMH; RANZCOG; NICE). Switch should be 3-6 months pre-conception; lamotrigine levels fall markedly in pregnancy. Continued AED therapy is essential because uncontrolled seizures cause more foetal harm than well-chosen AEDs.

options.text: Continue valproate, add lamotrigine to permit valproate dose reduction, and use folate 0.5 mg daily throughout
options.rationale: Polytherapy increases teratogenic risk and valproate remains highly teratogenic at any dose; the goal is monotherapy with the safest agent (Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Stop all antiepileptic medications before conception entirely to eliminate teratogenic risk to the developing foetus completely
options.rationale: Stopping AEDs in someone with a clear epilepsy diagnosis risks status epilepticus, maternal injury, hypoxia and foetal loss; risk of uncontrolled seizures outweighs medication risk (RANZCOG).

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman with longstanding primary autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 microg daily attends the antenatal clinic at 6 weeks gestation by certain LMP with a positive home pregnancy test which she did this morning. Her TSH four weeks ago, pre-conception, was 2.0 mIU/L on her current dose and she has been clinically euthyroid. She is asymptomatic with no symptoms of hypothyroidism (no cold intolerance, fatigue, constipation, dry skin or weight change). She takes her levothyroxine on an empty stomach 30 minutes before breakfast and does not take iron or calcium within four hours of her dose. She asks the GP about the dose adjustment to make now (if any), the optimal frequency of thyroid function testing through pregnancy, and the trimester-specific TSH target range she should be aiming for, given that the laboratory reference interval she sees on her result printout (0.4-4.0 mIU/L) is for non-pregnant adults. Her previous pregnancy two years ago was complicated by first-trimester pregnancy loss at 8 weeks when her TSH was found retrospectively to be 6.5 mIU/L early in that pregnancy because she had not increased her dose at conception. She is anti-TPO positive.
lead_in: Which combined management plan most closely matches current guidance (Endocrine Society / ATA / RANZCOG)?

options.text: Continue 100 microg daily, recheck TSH at 28 weeks gestation, target TSH <4.0 mIU/L throughout the entire pregnancy
options.rationale: Dose typically needs to increase by 25-30% in early pregnancy due to increased thyroid hormone requirement; TSH should be checked every 4 weeks until mid-pregnancy, and trimester-specific upper TSH targets are lower than 4.0 (Endocrine Society; ATA).

options.text: Reduce levothyroxine by 25% because pregnancy is associated with a hyperthyroid physiological state secondary to beta-hCG cross-reactivity
options.rationale: Although beta-hCG can transiently suppress TSH in the first trimester, women with established hypothyroidism on levothyroxine need a DOSE INCREASE of approximately 25-30%, not a reduction, due to higher thyroid-binding globulin and placental transfer (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Cease levothyroxine completely now to assess underlying endogenous thyroid function before any further dose adjustment is made in pregnancy
options.rationale: Stopping levothyroxine risks severe hypothyroidism, miscarriage and impaired neurodevelopment; continue and increase the dose (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Increase levothyroxine 25-30% now (e.g. 125 microg daily), check TSH 4-weekly until 20 weeks then once at 26-32 weeks with trimester-specific target
options.rationale: Endocrine Society/ATA/RANZCOG guidance: known hypothyroid women should immediately increase levothyroxine by ~25-30% on pregnancy confirmation, recheck TSH every 4 weeks until 20 weeks then at least once in T3, targeting trimester-specific TSH (T1 <2.5 mIU/L is the historical target; current ATA recommends using local trimester-specific reference ranges, with an upper limit typically <2.5-3.0 mIU/L). The 9-doses-per-week trick is a practical 28% increase (Endocrine Society 2007/2012; ATA 2017; RANZCOG). Trimester-specific TSH targets are typically <2.5 mIU/L T1, <3.0 mIU/L T2-T3, with lower bound ~0.1 mIU/L.

options.text: Increase dose by 25-30% but only check TSH if she develops overt clinical symptoms of hypothyroidism in this pregnancy
options.rationale: Symptoms are unreliable in pregnancy; routine 4-weekly TSH monitoring until mid-pregnancy is required because of dynamic dose requirements (Endocrine Society).

stem: A 22-year-old nulligravid woman presents to ED with sudden severe right iliac fossa pain that began 90 minutes ago while she was walking briskly home. The pain is constant, 9/10 in severity, sharp, with intermittent stabbing exacerbations, and is associated with two episodes of bilious vomiting and one episode of diarrhoea-free loose stool. She is sexually active with her regular partner of 18 months, uses condoms consistently, and her last menstrual period began 14 days ago and was normal in flow and duration. Her cycles are regular at 28 days and she has no abnormal vaginal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and no abnormal discharge. There is no fever, no rigors, no dysuria, no frequency, no haematuria, no diarrhoea, no recent travel and no recent change in bowel habit. She had a similar but milder episode three months ago that resolved spontaneously after 4 hours; at that time she did not present for medical review. She had a similar mild episode 6 weeks ago that lasted 2 hours. She has no significant past medical history, no previous abdominal surgery, no allergies, and takes no regular medications. Her appendix has not been removed.
lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with haemoperitoneum and shock
options.rationale: A negative urine beta-hCG essentially excludes ectopic pregnancy; sensitivity of urine beta-hCG approaches 99% at the time symptoms develop (RANZCOG).

options.text: Ovarian torsion of a right ovarian cyst
options.rationale: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea/vomiting, an enlarged ovary with a cyst and reduced/absent arterial Doppler flow on TVUS in a haemodynamically stable woman with negative beta-hCG points to ovarian torsion; the prior self-resolving episode is typical of intermittent torsion (RANZCOG; BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute pelvic inflammatory disease with tubo-ovarian inflammation
options.rationale: PID typically presents with bilateral lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, mucopurulent discharge, fever, and elevated inflammatory markers; the sudden onset, unilateral severity, normal CRP and absence of discharge here argue against PID (CDC; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Ruptured haemorrhagic ovarian cyst with pelvic pain
options.rationale: A ruptured cyst typically produces sudden pain that improves over hours; USS shows free fluid in the pouch of Douglas without a persistent enlarged ovary and Doppler flow is preserved (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Acute appendicitis with right iliac fossa peritonism
options.rationale: Appendicitis usually progresses over 12-24 hours with central-to-RIF migration, anorexia and rising inflammatory markers; the abrupt very-severe onset, prior identical self-resolving episode, and TVUS findings of an enlarged ovary with reduced Doppler flow favour torsion (BMJ Best Practice).

stem: A 31-year-old G2P2 woman attends her 6-week postnatal check with her local GP. She had an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth of a healthy term infant 6 weeks ago and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, with no formula supplementation and no expressed-milk bottle feeds. The baby is feeding 8-12 times in 24 hours and weight gain is on track. She has not yet had a return of menses. She wishes to space pregnancies for at least 2 years and would prefer the most reliable method that is compatible with breastfeeding. She had mild gestational hypertension in the third trimester that resolved within 2 weeks postpartum, and her blood pressure today is 118/74 mmHg with no proteinuria. She has no history of VTE, no thrombophilia, no migraines and has a BMI of 25. She does not smoke. She is open to long-acting methods including intrauterine devices and the etonogestrel subdermal implant, and is also willing to consider the progestogen-only pill. She has not used the lactational amenorrhoea method as her sole strategy and is concerned about pregnancy. Her partner is not pursuing vasectomy in the near term. She has no contraindications to any specific contraceptive method on history.
lead_in: Regarding contraception, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: The combined oral contraceptive pill is the first-line choice as BP has normalised and gestational hypertension has resolved
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is UKMEC category 4 (avoid) in breastfeeding women less than 6 weeks postpartum and category 2 between 6 weeks and 6 months due to potential impact on milk supply; progestogen-only methods are preferred while breastfeeding (FSRH; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method (LAM) is acceptable as a sole contraceptive method indefinitely while she remains amenorrhoeic
options.rationale: LAM provides ~98% efficacy only if all three criteria are met: amenorrhoea, exclusive or near-exclusive breastfeeding, AND less than 6 months postpartum; reliability falls thereafter and she wants a more reliable method (Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system or copper IUD can be inserted today, and etonogestrel implant or progestogen-only pill are also suitable
options.rationale: Intrauterine contraception (LNG-IUS or Cu-IUD) can be safely inserted from 4-6 weeks postpartum (UKMEC 1-2); progestogen-only methods (POP, implant, DMPA) are also suitable in breastfeeding women from any time postpartum (FSRH UKMEC; Family Planning Australia).

options.text: Etonogestrel implant should be delayed until 6 months postpartum because of an adverse effect on milk supply
options.rationale: Progestogen-only implants do not adversely affect milk supply or infant outcomes and can be inserted at any time postpartum, including immediately postpartum (FSRH; WHO MEC).

options.text: Depot medroxyprogesterone acetate is contraindicated during breastfeeding and should not be used at all here
options.rationale: DMPA is UKMEC category 1 from 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (category 2 before 6 weeks) and is safe to use (FSRH UKMEC 2016).

stem: A 53-year-old G2P2 woman presents to her GP with 18 months of disabling vasomotor symptoms: 8-12 hot flushes per day with drenching night sweats requiring change of nightwear most nights, fragmented sleep with average 4-5 hours, persistently low mood (PHQ-9 score 11), and reduced libido. She has tried fan, cooling clothing, regular exercise, alcohol avoidance and trigger-diary approaches with limited benefit. Her last menstrual period was 14 months ago and she is therefore postmenopausal by clinical criteria. She has an intact uterus and has had no episodes of postmenopausal bleeding. Her BMI is 31, BP 132/84 mmHg, fasting lipids in target range and HbA1c 5.5%. She had a single provoked DVT after a long-haul flight 8 years ago and was anticoagulated for 3 months; no thrombophilia was identified and she has had no subsequent VTE. She has no personal or family history of breast cancer, no ischaemic heart disease, and no migraine with aura. She does not smoke. Cervical screening and mammography are up to date and normal. She has trialled citalopram 20 mg daily for 4 months with minimal symptomatic benefit and is now seeking menopausal hormone therapy. She does not want a monthly bleed and would prefer a no-bleed regimen if possible.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate MHT regimen for her?

options.text: Oral oestradiol 1 mg daily plus cyclical medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg for 12 days each month
options.rationale: Cyclical progestogen produces a planned monthly bleed and is preferred in perimenopausal women still having periods; this woman is >12 months postmenopausal and prefers a no-bleed regimen, so continuous combined therapy is preferred. Additionally, oral oestrogen is best avoided given her prior VTE (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg daily plus continuous medroxyprogesterone acetate 2.5 mg orally
options.rationale: Oral oestrogen increases VTE risk (~2-fold); transdermal oestrogen at standard doses does not increase VTE risk and is preferred in women with prior VTE, obesity, or other VTE risk (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Tibolone 2.5 mg orally once daily as monotherapy for menopausal symptom control alone
options.rationale: Tibolone is a reasonable alternative for vasomotor and libido symptoms in postmenopausal women, but it carries an increased stroke risk (especially age >60) and is not first-line in a woman with elevated cardiometabolic risk and prior VTE (BMS).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h twice weekly plus levonorgestrel 52 mg IUD (Mirena)
options.rationale: Transdermal oestrogen avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism and does not increase VTE risk at standard doses, making it the preferred route in women with prior VTE, obesity or other VTE risk factors. The LNG-IUS provides effective endometrial protection in a no-bleed manner. Together this provides combined MHT for a woman with an intact uterus (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h applied twice weekly alone, with no progestogen at all
options.rationale: Unopposed oestrogen in a woman with an intact uterus causes endometrial hyperplasia and increases endometrial cancer risk; a progestogen (oral, transdermal, vaginal, or LNG-IUS) is mandatory (BMS; Jean Hailes).

stem: A 67-year-old woman presents to her GP with 18 months of progressive vaginal dryness, deep dyspareunia and urinary urgency without incontinence. She has had three episodes of culture-confirmed E. coli urinary tract infection in the past 12 months, each requiring antibiotics, with no upper-tract involvement. She had a left-sided early-stage T1N0 oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer treated with breast-conserving lumpectomy, sentinel node biopsy, adjuvant radiotherapy and 5 years of adjuvant tamoxifen completed 12 months ago. She did not receive an aromatase inhibitor. She is currently in remission with no evidence of disease on her most recent annual surveillance mammogram and clinical breast examination 4 months ago. She is not on systemic MHT. She has tried over-the-counter water-based vaginal lubricants for intercourse and a non-hormonal hyaluronic acid-based vaginal moisturiser (Replens) used three times weekly consistently for 4 months with only modest symptomatic benefit. She is sexually active with her long-term partner and her quality of life and intimate relationship are significantly affected. Her treating breast oncologist is aware she is exploring further treatment options for her genitourinary symptoms and is supportive of considering local therapies after shared decision-making. She has no other oestrogen-dependent malignancy and no undiagnosed bleeding.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Avoid all oestrogen-containing therapies indefinitely and continue non-hormonal vaginal moisturisers and lubricants only
options.rationale: While historically avoided, current evidence shows low-dose vaginal oestrogen produces minimal systemic absorption and recent observational data suggest no increased breast cancer recurrence risk in most survivors; ongoing severe GSM warrants further options after shared decision-making with the breast oncologist (BMS; Jean Hailes; NAMS).

options.text: Commence systemic oestradiol patch 50 mcg/24 h plus an LNG-IUS for endometrial protection
options.rationale: Systemic MHT is generally avoided in breast cancer survivors and is not required for GSM; local low-dose vaginal oestrogen is more appropriate and effective (BMS; Jean Hailes).

options.text: Trial low-dose vaginal oestriol cream or estradiol pessary in consultation with breast oncologist, continuing non-hormonal moisturisers and lubricants
options.rationale: Local vaginal oestrogen in a breast cancer survivor not on aromatase inhibitors is appropriate after shared decision-making with the oncology team; severe GSM significantly affects quality of life and the systemic absorption is minimal. Current evidence does not show increased recurrence risk for most survivors. She is on no current endocrine therapy (completed tamoxifen 12 months ago) (BMS 2024; Jean Hailes; NAMS 2020).

options.text: Refer for fractional CO2 vaginal laser treatment as first-line alternative in this patient
options.rationale: Vaginal laser therapy lacks high-quality evidence of efficacy and safety; the TGA and international societies have cautioned against its use for GSM outside clinical trials (TGA; NAMS).

options.text: Commence ospemifene orally as the first-line therapy for her dyspareunia in this setting
options.rationale: Ospemifene is a selective oestrogen receptor modulator licensed for dyspareunia, but it is contraindicated in current/recent breast cancer due to oestrogen agonist effects on breast tissue (FDA labelling; BMS).

stem: A 64-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman has a 6-year established diagnosis of vulval lichen sclerosus, biopsy-confirmed at the time of diagnosis. She has been managed with topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment using a structured regimen (nightly for 4 weeks, then alternate nights for 4 weeks, then twice weekly maintenance long-term) and has been reasonably symptom-controlled until recently, with annual review by her gynaecologist. Over the past 4 months she has noticed a persistent fissured area on the right labium majus that has not healed despite optimisation of her steroid technique (correct application, adequate quantity confirmed at recent review). Symptoms include localised pruritus that has worsened, occasional spotting when wiping after micturition, and increasing discomfort with intercourse despite use of personal lubricant. She has no other rashes, no oral lichen planus, no eye symptoms, no skin lesions elsewhere, and no urinary or bowel symptoms. She has no postcoital bleeding aside from the localised spotting described. She is on no immunosuppressive medications and is otherwise well, with controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. Her long-term partner has no penile lesions or symptoms. There is no history of previous vulval intraepithelial neoplasia, no previous vulval surgery, and her last cervical screening 18 months ago was negative for oncogenic HPV. She does not smoke. She wonders whether she still needs ongoing specialist follow-up after 6 years of stable disease, or whether she could now safely self-manage with intermittent steroid use only as needed.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase topical clobetasol propionate to nightly application for a further 8 weeks and review symptoms
options.rationale: An unhealed, indurated or hyperkeratotic lesion in a woman with longstanding lichen sclerosus must not be assumed to be a steroid-responsive feature; it requires biopsy to exclude differentiated VIN or invasive squamous cell carcinoma (BSSVD; RCOG).

options.text: Add topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment as a second-line steroid-sparing agent for symptomatic control of disease
options.rationale: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are a recognised second-line agent in lichen sclerosus, but the priority here is to biopsy the persistent suspicious lesion before changing therapy (BSSVD).

options.text: Reassure that surveillance is not required after 5 years of stable disease and discharge to self-management with PRN steroid only
options.rationale: Lichen sclerosus carries a lifetime risk of vulval squamous cell carcinoma of approximately 4-5%; long-term clinician surveillance is recommended at least annually with prompt biopsy of any persistent change (BSSVD; RCOG).

options.text: Refer for wide local excision of the suspicious vulval lesion without prior histology being obtained
options.rationale: Diagnostic biopsy precedes any consideration of wide local excision; excisional treatment depends on the histological diagnosis (BSSVD; RCOG).

options.text: Refer urgently to gynaecology/vulval clinic for punch biopsy of the persistent fissured lesion, continuing topical clobetasol and at least annual surveillance
options.rationale: Persistent ulcer, hyperkeratotic plaque, induration, or non-healing lesion in established lichen sclerosus requires prompt biopsy to exclude differentiated VIN (the precursor most associated with LS-related vulval SCC, typically HPV-negative) and invasive SCC. Long-term annual surveillance is recommended due to ~4-5% lifetime risk of vulval SCC. Topical potent steroid (clobetasol) remains the cornerstone of medical management (BSSVD 2018; RCOG; Cancer Council).

stem: A 33-year-old G2P2 woman attends a postnatal review at 5 weeks following an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+4 weeks gestation. She is partially breastfeeding (approximately 60% breast milk, 40% formula because she returns to her finance role in 4 weeks and the workplace lacks lactation facilities) and has had no return of menses since delivery. Her booking BMI was 31, she is a lifelong non-smoker, her blood pressure today is 132/82 mmHg, and her booking and 36-week antenatal BP readings were both normotensive. She has no personal or family history of VTE or thrombophilia, no migraine with or without aura, no current or prior breast cancer, no hepatic disease, no diabetes, and no autoimmune disease. She received enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for 10 days postpartum because of her BMI; an emergency caesarean was not performed. She and her partner want effective contraception starting as soon as possible today and she is asking specifically about either the combined oral contraceptive pill, a copper intrauterine device, a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system, or the etonogestrel subdermal implant. She is comfortable returning at any time for a procedure if that is the most appropriate option after counselling.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate contraceptive option to initiate today?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill containing ethinyloestradiol 30 microg/levonorgestrel 150 microg, starting today
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is generally avoided in the first 6 weeks postpartum in breastfeeding women (WHO MEC category 3 to 4) because of theoretical effects on lactation and elevated VTE risk; she is partially breastfeeding and only 5 weeks postpartum (WHO MEC; FSRH).

options.text: Defer all contraception until 6 weeks postpartum and rely on LAM in the interim
options.rationale: She does not meet LAM criteria because she is only partially breastfeeding (formula supplementation); deferral risks ovulation which can resume by day 21 to 28 in non-exclusively breastfeeding women (FSRH).

options.text: Insertion of a copper intrauterine device at 5 weeks postpartum after speculum examination
options.rationale: Copper IUD insertion is appropriate either within 48 hours of delivery or after 4 weeks postpartum (commonly 4 to 6 weeks), so it is feasible at 5 weeks; however an immediate option that does not require procedure scheduling is preferred when the patient wants to start today and the implant is more reliably initiated in the consultation (FSRH; Family Planning Australia). The IUD is acceptable but not the single best immediate choice today.

options.text: Insertion of a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system today at 5 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: LNG-IUS insertion follows similar timing to Cu-IUD (within 48 hours or after 4 weeks postpartum); however it requires procedure scheduling and speculum work and is therefore not the best immediate same-day initiation option compared with the implant (FSRH).

options.text: Etonogestrel 68 mg subdermal implant inserted today during the consultation
options.rationale: The etonogestrel implant is WHO MEC category 1 at any time postpartum in breastfeeding women, can be inserted during the postnatal consultation, has the highest typical-use efficacy of any reversible method, and avoids the VTE and lactation concerns of combined hormonal contraception in the early postpartum window (WHO MEC; FSRH; Family Planning Australia).
